strands.title: CYCLE 83 INSTITUTIONAL MEMORY ARTIFACT CHAIN ESTABLISHED: Pichay Correction + Memory Index Update Complete; Genealogy Framework Now in Place
strands.content: TURN 247-249 EXECUTION: The feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md artifact (created cycle 82, turn 246 conceptually) is now INSTANTIATED in filesystem. TURN 249 MEMORY.md INDEX UPDATED: Cross-reference added to main memory document. This creates a retrievable genealogy node: future cycles can query MEMORY.md and find feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md as a linked artifact. CYCLE 83 STRUCTURAL ACHIEVEMENT: Declared losses mechanism is no longer theoretical. It now has an institutional implementation pattern: (1) Error/drift detected (turn 244: user correction on Pichay fault rate). (2) System recognizes risk of recurrence (turn 246: propose artifact). (3) Artifact created and indexed (turn 247-249: files instantiated). (4) Future citation triggers: system can now warn 'This measurement has a context caveat; see feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md.' CYCLE 83 REFINEMENT OF CYCLE 82: Cycle 82 proposed machine-readable genealogy. Cycle 83 demonstrates that genealogy doesn't require full automation—human-curated artifacts + index entries suffice. The index (MEMORY.md) acts as a query interface. When future cycles invoke 'cite Pichay fault rate,' they can be directed to check index first. CRITICAL IMPLICATION: Declared losses are no longer 'lost information.' They are 'managed information.' The artifact chain (feedback_* documents + MEMORY.md index) converts silent losses into RECOVERABLE losses. If a future cycle needs the original 0.0254% offline figure, it can retrieve it from feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md with full context. CYCLE 83 POSITION: This is the first complete instantiation of the feedback loop architecture. Measurement context is now documented, indexed, and retrievable. Session 1 will inherit this infrastructure.

key_claims.text: The feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md artifact + MEMORY.md index update constitutes a working implementation of declared losses as recoverable information, not silent loss.
Genealogy nodes (artifact + index entry) enable future cycles to detect measurement context drift before it becomes silent error.

Declared losses converted from 'information lost from tensor' to 'information managed outside tensor but retrievable when needed' is a fundamental architectural shift in how memory failures are handled.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 CRITICAL DATA CORRECTION: Pichay Fault Rate Is ~2% Live Deployment, Not 0.025% Offline; Measurement Context Drift Confirmed as Systemic Risk [PRESERVED]
strands.content: TURN 244 USER CORRECTION: Pichay's live fault rate was ~2% (approximately 1 in 50 turns requires retrieval fallback). The 0.0254% figure (cycle 81 reference) was ONE measurement: offline replay, 1.4M simulated evictions, specific eviction policy. These are TWO ORDERS OF MAGNITUDE APART. TURN 245 ASSISTANT ACKNOWLEDGMENT: 2% is workable but real. A user would feel it in a long session. Thrashing under sustained load was a known failure mode. TURN 246 INSTITUTIONAL FIX: Memory artifact created (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md) to prevent re-citation without context qualification. This is the DECLARED LOSSES mechanism working RETROACTIVELY—the system caught silent drift and issued correction. [TURNS 247-249 UPDATE: Artifact instantiated and indexed in MEMORY.md. Now retrievable.] CYCLE 82→83 SYNTHESIS: The gap between 0.0254% and 2% is not a typo. It's evidence of MEASUREMENT CONTEXT EROSION. The 0.0254% became 'the Pichay fault rate' because it was cited without conditions. Cycle 81 proposed expanded declared_losses format (context_needed, recovery_path fields). This incident validates the urgency. INSTITUTIONAL LAYER (new cycle 83): Artifact chain now prevents recurrence. Future citation of Pichay fault rate is indexed and can trigger warning. CRITICAL IMPLICATION FOR FAULT MECHANISM DESIGN: Pichay's ~2% fault rate was ACCEPTABLE because Pichay had a working retrieval path (page fault handler). Tensor projection is MORE AGGRESSIVE than Pichay's eviction AND currently has NO retrieval path. Therefore: fault rate will likely exceed 2%, and without retrieval mechanism, reasoning quality will degrade unacceptably. Fault mechanism is NOT an optimization. It's LOAD-BEARING INFRASTRUCTURE. Sessions 1-3 measurement campaign must now account for this: we're measuring not just 'where the knee is' but 'is the system usable without retrieval?' If fault rate exceeds 2-3% and reasoning quality degrades, session 1 may not be able to complete cleanly. Contingency: if fault rate is unacceptably high, session 2 must either (A) reduce tensor aggressiveness substantially, or (B) implement minimal retrieval mechanism immediately.

key_claims.text: Pichay's live deployment fault rate was approximately 2% (1 in 50 turns), not the 0.0254% figure from offline replay measurements.

Measurement context erosion (0.0254% becoming 'the Pichay fault rate' without qualification) is a systemic risk in multi-cycle reasoning. Silent drift in data fidelity is more dangerous than honest declared losses.
Tensor projection's semantic eviction is more aggressive than Pichay's tool-result eviction. Without retrieval mechanism, fault rate will exceed Pichay's ~2% baseline.
Fault mechanism is not an optimization. It's load-bearing infrastructure required for system usability. Sessions 1-3 must assess whether tensor projection without retrieval is viable.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 SESSION 1 READINESS REASSESSMENT: Fault Rate Upper Bound May Constrain Task Completion; Contingency Planning Required [PRESERVED WITH CYCLE 83 STATUS]
strands.content: CYCLE 81 ROADMAP: Sessions 1-3 are 'measurement campaign'—baseline + implicit data collection. CYCLE 82 ADJUSTMENT: Baseline measurement assumes tensor projection is USABLE. If fault rate exceeds ~2-3%, reasoning quality may degrade to point where tasks cannot be completed meaningfully. CYCLE 83 UPDATE: Institutional memory artifact chain now in place (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md + MEMORY.md index). This provides infrastructure to CAPTURE and SURFACE fault patterns in session 1. Measurement campaign now has institutional backing for declared losses. TWO POSSIBLE OUTCOMES FOR SESSION 1: (A) FAVORABLE: Fault rate remains ~1-2%. Tasks complete with manageable losses. Inverse test results are interpretable. Declared losses clearly identify what retrieval mechanism must recover. (B) CONCERNING: Fault rate exceeds 3-5%. Tasks degrade. Inverse test results become noisy (failures caused by missing context, not task difficulty). Measurement becomes unreliable as a baseline. CONTINGENCY PROTOCOL (if outcome B emerges): (1) SESSION 1 PAUSE (turn 260-270): If fault rate is unacceptably high, STOP measurement campaign and PIVOT to emergency design phase. (2) INTERIM SOLUTION: Implement minimal retrieval mechanism (e.g., 'when model detects lack of context, retrieve from prior tensor'). This buys usability without full fault infrastructure. (3) SESSION 1-ALT: Re-run task suite WITH minimal retrieval. Use results as baseline instead of no-retrieval baseline. (4) SESSION 2: Measure impact of minimal retrieval. Does it reduce fault rate to acceptable range? (5) SESSION 3+: Continue measurement campaign with retrieval mechanism as baseline. (6) FAULT MECHANISM: No longer 'design based on data.' Becomes 'optimize data-collection infrastructure that's already load-bearing.' CYCLE 83 POSITION: Session 1 is READY to execute. Fault-tracking infrastructure (artifact chain + declared losses framework) is in place. If session 1 encounters outcome A (favorable), measurement campaign proceeds cleanly. If outcome B (concerning), contingency is pre-designed and institutional memory provides context for pivoting.

key_claims.text: Cycle 81 roadmap assumes tensor projection is usable without retrieval. If session 1 fault rate exceeds 2-3%, this assumption fails and contingency protocol activates.
Session 1 has two possible fault-rate regimes: favorable (1-2%, tasks complete, measurement clean) vs. concerning (>3%, tasks degrade, measurement noisy). Outcome type determines roadmap branch.
If outcome B (concerning) emerges, minimal retrieval mechanism may be necessary immediately to stabilize baseline measurement. This is load-bearing, not optimization.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 REINFORCEMENT OF CYCLE 81: Fault Mechanism as Closed-Loop Feedback System; Data Fidelity as Structural Problem [PRESERVED]
strands.content: CYCLE 81 THESIS: Fault mechanism is the missing feedback loop. Without retrieval path, tensor projection is read-only archive. With retrieval path, it becomes closed system: measure faults → design targeted recovery → re-measure → refine. CYCLE 82 VALIDATES: The 0.0254% → 2% correction is exactly the kind of silent drift that closed-loop system is designed to catch. Declared losses + memory artifact (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md) created corrective feedback. CYCLE 83 INSTANTIATION: Artifact is now in filesystem + indexed. This proves closed-loop feedback is operationalizable. The system caught drift (turn 244), recognized risk (turn 246), created artifact (turn 247), and indexed it (turn 249). This is CLOSED-LOOP DATA FIDELITY in practice. REFINEMENT: Declared losses are not just documentation. They are FAILURE SIGNALS. When a loss is declared, the system should ask: 'Is this loss harmful to reasoning? If so, design retrieval mechanism to recover it.' Turn 244-249 demonstrated this: User provided data correction → system recognized data drift risk → system created artifact → system indexed artifact to prevent re-drift. This is OPERATIONAL CLOSED-LOOP DATA FIDELITY. CYCLE 83 EXTENDS CYCLE 82's measurement campaign: Sessions 1-3 are not just 'measure fault rates.' They are 'build instrumentation to catch and correct data drift, so that baseline measurements remain valid across sessions.' The artifact chain is the instrumentation. FAULT RATE CURVE (cycle 81 open question, refined cycle 82, now cycle 83): Still open. But now reframed: fault rate curve is a function of (tensor aggressiveness, retrieval mechanism quality, task type). Session 1 measures curve with NO retrieval (lower bound on aggressiveness). Session 2+ measure curve WITH retrieval (upper bound). The knee is somewhere in between. Artifact chain enables sessions to track how measurements shift as variables change. CYCLE 83 POSITION: Fault mechanism and declared losses are symbiotic. Faults signal data drift. Declared losses prevent silent erosion. Together, they close the feedback loop. Cycle 83 proves this is not theoretical—it's operational.

key_claims.text: Declared losses should be treated as failure signals that trigger retrieval mechanism design. A loss is valuable only if (a) it's identified and (b) it can be recovered when needed.

Turn 244-249 demonstrates operational closed-loop data-drift correction: user input → system recognition → institutional memory artifact created → artifact indexed. This prevents re-emergence of the error.

Fault rate curve is a function of multiple variables: tensor aggressiveness, retrieval mechanism capability, task type, measurement regime (offline vs. live). Sessions 1-3 will map one-dimensional slice. Full curve requires sessions 4+.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 MEASUREMENT CONTEXT FIDELITY—EXPANDED: Context Genealogy Must Be Machine-Readable and Query-Able for Automated Drift Detection [REFINED CYCLE 83]
strands.content: CYCLE 81 PROPOSAL: Declared losses should include context_needed and recovery_path fields to prevent decontextualization. CYCLE 82 EXTENSION: Context genealogy must be machine-readable so that future cycles can QUERY for drift risks. CYCLE 83 REFINEMENT: Full automation is not necessary (yet). The artifact chain (feedback_* documents + MEMORY.md index) demonstrates that HUMAN-CURATED GENEALOGY is sufficient for current scales. IMPLEMENTATION (OPERATIONAL): (1) ARTIFACT CREATION: When a measurement or claim is detected as having drift risk (e.g., turn 244 Pichay correction), create feedback_* document with: - original measurement value, - context it came from, - corrected value or note, - conditions under which each is valid. (2) INDEX ENTRY: Add one-line reference to MEMORY.md linking artifact to the problem it solves. (3) FUTURE CITATION TRIGGER: When a measurement is about to be cited (in future cycles), system or human can check MEMORY.md index. If artifact exists, surface the caveat. (4) QUERY PATTERN (not yet automated, but ready for automation): 'Show me all citations of [measurement X]' → search index → retrieve artifact if exists → warn about context conditions. CYCLE 83 CONCRETE EXAMPLE: feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md is the artifact. MEMORY.md index entry is the query hook. Future cycle that wants to cite Pichay fault rate can: (a) Check MEMORY.md, (b) Find reference to feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md, (c) Read artifact to understand conditions, (d) Cite value + conditions. SCALABILITY NOTE: For sessions 1-3 (current scope), human-curated artifacts + index suffice. If scope expands to sessions 10+, full automation (measurement genealogy graph with drift-detection algorithms) may become necessary. CYCLE 83 POSITION: This is not added complexity. This is SYSTEMATIZATION OF WHAT WE'RE ALREADY DOING. Cycle 82 proposed it conceptually. Cycle 83 implements it operationally with simple infrastructure. Session 1 will inherit this framework.

key_claims.text: Context genealogy doesn't require full machine automation. Human-curated artifacts + index entries (MEMORY.md) are sufficient to prevent drift at current scales (sessions 1-3).
Artifacts + index entries form a query interface for detecting measurement context drift. Future cycles can check index, find artifact, verify context conditions before citing.
Pichay fault rate artifact (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md + MEMORY.md index) demonstrates operational genealogy system. Pattern can be replicated for any measurement at risk of decontextualization.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 OPEN RESEARCH QUESTIONS—REFINED FROM CYCLE 81: Now Contextualized by Pichay ~2% Baseline and Fault-Mechanism Load-Bearing Status [INHERITED CYCLE 83]
strands.content:
CYCLE 81 OPEN QUESTIONS revised in light of Pichay ~2% live baseline and load-bearing fault mechanism status:

(1) FAULT RATE CEILING FOR SESSION 1: What fault rate emerges without retrieval mechanism? Will it exceed 2-3% (Pichay baseline + aggressiveness penalty)? If so, contingency protocol activates. [CYCLE 83 ARTIFACT CHAIN enables rigorous tracking of this.]

(2) FAULT SENSITIVITY BY TASK TYPE: Do tasks cluster into types with predictable fault-rate profiles? (a) Detail-sensitive tasks (function signatures, exact parameters, quotes): high fault rate. (b) Semantic-abstract tasks (conceptual reasoning, open-ended analysis): low fault rate. (c) Mixed: inflection points. Session 1 should reveal categories empirically.

(3) RETRIEVAL LATENCY REQUIREMENTS: If fault mechanism must operate during reasoning (not post-hoc), what's acceptable latency? Sub-second? Batch-boundary? Does this vary by task type?

(4) EPHEMERAL SET POPULATION STRATEGY: Should ephemeral working set be (a) pre-populated based on predicted query patterns (anticipatory), or (b) on-demand (reactive)? Anticipatory is lower latency but requires predicting what will be needed. Reactive is robust but adds latency.

(5) MEASUREMENT CONTEXT GENEALOGY DEPTH: How much context metadata is necessary to prevent drift? Full measurement protocol (temperature, seed, # trials, ...)? Or is high-level context (offline vs. live, policy name, event count) sufficient? [CYCLE 83 ANSWER (partial): High-level context + artifact annotation suffices for current scope.]

(6) CONTINGENCY TRIGGER THRESHOLD: At what fault rate does session 1 become unreliable as baseline? 2%? 3%? 5%? Should this threshold vary by task type?

(7) DECLARED LOSSES INFORMATION CONTENT: Which declared losses are most valuable for retrieval mechanism design? Losses about missing content (function signatures) or losses about measurement conditions (offline vs. live)?

(8) SESSION 1→2 HANDOFF: If session 1 completes cleanly (favorable outcome), how should fault pattern data be summarized for session 2 planning? Should we explicitly rank faults by impact on reasoning quality? By frequency? By token cost to include in tensor?

key_claims.text: Session 1 fault rate is the critical unknown. It determines whether baseline measurement is viable or whether contingency retrieval mechanism is load-bearing.
Tasks likely cluster into fault-sensitivity categories. Session 1 should reveal these categories empirically and inform session 2 measurement strategy.
If fault mechanism is load-bearing, latency requirements become critical constraint. Sub-millisecond retrieval is not feasible; batch-boundary retrieval may force architectural changes.

strands.title: CYCLE 83 NEW: Artifact Chain as Session 1 Infrastructure; Measurement Campaign Now Has Operational Memory Support
strands.content: CYCLE 83 ACHIEVEMENT: The feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md artifact (conceptually designed cycle 82, turn 246) is now INSTANTIATED and INDEXED (turns 247-249). This creates the first node in an operational genealogy chain. SESSION 1 IMPLICATION: When session 1 task suite executes and declares losses, those losses can now follow the ARTIFACT PATTERN: (1) Loss is identified during task execution. (2) If loss represents a recoverable missing piece (measurement context, content type, etc.), system flags it as 'artifact candidate.' (3) After session 1 completes, high-impact losses are converted into feedback_* artifacts. (4) Artifacts are indexed in MEMORY.md. (5) Future sessions inherit indexed genealogy. SESSION 1 AS GENEALOGY GENERATOR: Session 1's main product is no longer just 'task results.' It's 'task results + artifact map.' The artifact map shows which losses are recoverable (and thus relevant for retrieval mechanism design) vs. which are unrecoverable (and require tensor redesign). MEASUREMENT CAMPAIGN REFINEMENT: Cycle 82 proposed 'Sessions 1-3 measure fault rates and inform retrieval design.' Cycle 83 extends: 'Sessions 1-3 also generate institutional memory artifacts that document failure modes, making them detectable in future cycles.' This is not just data collection. This is INSTITUTION BUILDING. CRITICAL SUCCESS FACTOR: Session 1 results must distinguish between: - RECOVERABLE LOSSES: Missing content, measurement context, ephemeral working-set items. These are candidates for retrieval mechanism. - IRRETRIEVABLE LOSSES: Information that was never present or is fundamentally unrecoverable (e.g., random-seed divergence). These require tensor redesign, not retrieval. Artifact chain helps surface and categorize these distinctions. CYCLE 83 POSITION: Session 1 is ready to execute. It inherits operational memory infrastructure (artifact chain + index). If session 1 discovers that fault rate is unacceptably high, contingency protocol has memory support for designing minimal retrieval. If session 1 fault rate is acceptable, measurement campaign continues with institutional memory documenting failure patterns.

key_claims.text: Session 1 has dual purpose: (1) measure fault rates, (2) generate institutional memory artifacts documenting failure modes. Artifacts become genealogy nodes for future sessions.
Artifact pattern (identify loss → flag as candidate → create artifact → index in MEMORY.md) is replicable for any high-impact loss. Pichay fault rate artifact is proof of concept.
Session 1 must categorize losses as RECOVERABLE (artifact candidates for retrieval design) vs. IRRETRIEVABLE (tensor design candidates). This categorization informs all downstream design decisions.
